webmd.author: WebMD
webmd.title: Bitter Melon: Overview, Uses, Side Effects, Precautions, Interactions

actions.action: Antidiabetic / blood-sugar lowering (improves glucose uptake and GLUT4 translocation)
Bitter digestive / stomachic (traditional use for poor digestion); mild laxative
Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory

constituents.name: Cucurbitane-type triterpenoids (momordicosides)
constituents.note: Now regarded as the main antidiabetic principles.

constituents.name: Charantin
constituents.note: A mixture of two sterol glucosides, historically credited with the hypoglycaemic effect.

constituents.name: Polypeptide-p ('plant insulin')
constituents.note: An insulin-like polypeptide of the fruit and seed.

constituents.name: Vicine (in the seeds)
constituents.note: A pyrimidine glycoside that can trigger favism in G6PD-deficient people.

contraindications.note: Avoid in pregnancy: bitter melon can stimulate the uterus and has been associated with miscarriage; also avoid during breastfeeding.
Strong blood-sugar-lowering action: used with insulin or other diabetes medicines it can cause hypoglycaemia - monitor blood glucose and seek medical advice.

People with glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency should avoid the seeds, which contain vicine and can cause favism (haemolytic anaemia). Excessive intake may cause abdominal pain and diarrhoea.

internal_notes: Enriched 2026-06-12 from scientific and general literature (Frontiers in Pharmacology and Int J Mol Sci reviews; WebMD safety monograph). Originally a skeleton imported from the omniasana.bio Symptom-to-Plant Lookup tool (blood-sugar, indigestion); associations retained and now part-mapped and cited.

castelo2020.author: Castelo-Branco, C., Gambacciani, M., Cano, A., Minkin, M.J., Rachon, D., Ruan, X., Beer, A.M., Schnitker, J., Henneicke-von Zepelin, H.H. and Pickartz, S.
castelo2020.title: Review & meta-analysis: isopropanolic black cohosh extract iCR for menopausal symptoms - an update on the evidence

fritz2013.author: Fritz, H., Seely, D., McGowan, J., Skidmore, B., Fernandes, R., Kennedy, D.A., Cooley, K., Wong, R., Sagar, S., Balneaves, L.G. and Fergusson, D.
fritz2013.title: Black cohosh and breast cancer: a systematic review

actions.action: Relief of menopausal symptoms - hot flashes, sweating, sleep and mood disturbance
Reduces neurovegetative and psychological menopausal symptoms (including anxiety and low mood)
Premenstrual and menstrual complaints (traditional use)

constituents.name: Triterpene glycosides (actein, 23-epi-26-deoxyactein/cimicifugoside)
constituents.note: The compounds to which standardised extracts are normalised; act without measurable systemic oestrogenic effect.

contraindications.note: Rare reports of liver injury have prompted a precaution; large meta-analytic data found no evidence of hepatotoxicity, but stop use and seek advice if signs of liver problems occur (jaundice, dark urine, upper-abdominal pain).

Current evidence does not show an increased risk of breast cancer or proliferation of oestrogen-sensitive tissue, but women with or at risk of hormone-sensitive cancer should use it only under medical guidance.
Not recommended during pregnancy or breastfeeding.

internal_notes: Enriched 2026-06-12 from scientific literature (Climacteric meta-analysis; Integrative Cancer Therapies systematic review). Actaea racemosa = Cimicifuga racemosa. Originally a skeleton imported from the omniasana.bio Symptom-to-Plant Lookup tool (menopause, hot-flashes, pms, anxiety); associations retained and now part-mapped and cited.

review2014.title: Rubus fruticosus (blackberry) use as an herbal medicine

grabek2022.author: Grabek-Lejko, D., Milek, M., Sidor, E., Puchalski, C. and Dzugan, M.
grabek2022.title: Antiviral and Antibacterial Effect of Honey Enriched with Rubus spp. as a Functional Food with Enhanced Antioxidant Properties

actions.action: Astringent for mild diarrhoea (tannins tighten the gut lining and reduce excess secretion)
Anti-inflammatory gargle / mouthwash for sore throat and oral mucosa inflammation
Antioxidant
Antibacterial

constituents.name: Tannins (gallotannins and ellagitannins)
constituents.note: The astringent principles of the leaf.

constituents.name: Flavonoids and phenolic acids (including gallic acid)
Vitamin C and minerals (iron)
constituents.note: Minor nutritive constituents of the leaf.

contraindications.note: Tannin-rich: high or prolonged intake may cause stomach upset or constipation and can reduce the absorption of iron and some medicines taken at the same time.
Traditional use only; diarrhoea that persists (especially in children) needs medical assessment and attention to rehydration.

internal_notes: Enriched 2026-06-12 from scientific and general literature (Pharmacognosy Reviews herbal-medicine review; Molecules antioxidant/antibacterial study). Note: Rubus fruticosus is an aggregate of many microspecies. Originally a skeleton imported from the omniasana.bio Symptom-to-Plant Lookup tool (diarrhea, sore-throat, inflammation); associations retained and now part-mapped and cited.

zietal2024.author: Zietal, K., Mirowska-Guzel, D., Nowaczyk, A. and Blecharz-Klin, K.
zietal2024.title: Cnicus benedictus: Folk Medicinal Uses, Biological Activities, and In Silico Screening of Main Phytochemical Constituents

actions.action: Bitter digestive / stomachic for indigestion and dyspepsia (stimulates saliva and gastric secretion)
Appetite stimulant for loss of appetite (approved use under German Commission E)
Galactagogue to support breast-milk supply (traditional; no valid clinical-trial evidence)
Antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory (attributed to cnicin)

constituents.name: Sesquiterpene lactones (cnicin, arctigenin, arctiin)
constituents.note: Cnicin is the principal bitter principle and main bioactive compound.

contraindications.note: As a member of the daisy family (Asteraceae/ragweed), it can cause allergic reactions in sensitive people; high doses reportedly cause stomach cramps, nausea and vomiting.
Avoid during pregnancy because of a traditional uterine-stimulating reputation.
Although traditionally used to support milk supply, there is no scientifically valid clinical evidence for the galactagogue use.

internal_notes: Enriched 2026-06-12 from scientific and general literature (Planta Medica review; Drugs.com and LactMed monographs). Cnicus benedictus = Centaurea benedicta. Originally a skeleton imported from the omniasana.bio Symptom-to-Plant Lookup tool (indigestion, appetite-loss, lactation-support); associations retained and now part-mapped and cited.

yu2020.author: Yu, G., Xiang, W., Zhang, T., Zeng, L., Yang, K. and Li, J.
yu2020.title: Effectiveness of Boswellia and Boswellia extract for osteoarthritis patients: a systematic review and meta-analysis

ammon2016.author: Ammon, H.P.T.
ammon2016.title: Boswellic Acids and Their Role in Chronic Inflammatory Diseases
ammon2016.journal: Advances in Experimental Medicine and Biology

actions.action: Anti-inflammatory (inhibits 5-lipoxygenase / leukotriene synthesis and NF-kB signalling)
Osteoarthritis: reduces joint pain and stiffness and improves function (over at least 4 weeks)
Supports inflammatory arthritis including rheumatoid arthritis
Analgesic (pain relief)
Supportive in inflammatory bowel disease (ulcerative colitis, Crohn's disease)
Supportive in bronchial asthma

constituents.name: Boswellic acids (pentacyclic triterpenes, especially KBA and AKBA)
constituents.note: The principal anti-inflammatory compounds; AKBA inhibits 5-lipoxygenase. Absorption improves when taken with a high-fat meal.

constituents.name: Other triterpenes and essential oil of the resin

contraindications.note: Generally well tolerated; the most commonly reported side effects are mild gastrointestinal symptoms, and allergic reactions are rare.
Boswellic acids can affect drug-metabolising enzymes, so caution is advised when taking other medicines; safety in pregnancy and breastfeeding is not established, so avoid medicinal doses.

internal_notes: Enriched 2026-06-12 from scientific literature (BMC Complementary Medicine and Therapies osteoarthritis meta-analysis; review of boswellic acids in chronic inflammatory disease). Originally a skeleton imported from the omniasana.bio Symptom-to-Plant Lookup tool (inflammation, arthritis, asthma, pain, ibd); associations retained and now part-mapped and cited.
